coredata.dc:title: Enhanced detection sensitivity of Leptospira DNA using a post-deposition annealed carbon quantum dots integrated tapered optical fiber biosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Zainuddin, Nurul Hida
Chee, Hui Yee
Abdul Rashid, Suraya
Ahmad, Muhammad Zamharir
Zan, Zuraidah
Abu Bakar, Muhammad Hafiz
Alresheedi, Mohammed Thamer
Mahdi, Mohd Adzir
Yaacob, Mohd Hanif
coredata.dc:description: Leptospirosis is an illness caused by a Leptospira bacterial infection, and it can be fatal if not correctly diagnosed and treated. This work explores the development of an optical biosensor that utilizes tapered optical fiber integrated with post-deposition annealed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to improve Leptospira deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) detection. A standard optical single-mode fiber was tapered before being deposited with CQDs. The surface properties of CQDs on the tapered optical fiber were then tuned using a simple, one-step annealing process. Leptospira probe DNA was covalently bonded to the annealed CQDs surface using several commercially available linkers. The detection was achieved through probe DNA fragments that were hybridized into different concentrations of complementary target DNA. The biosensor limit of detection remained at 1.0 fM, but a better sensitivity of 2.321 ± 0.048 nm/nM was obtained. The affinity towards Leptospira complementary DNA improved as the equilibrium dissociation constant value was lowered to 0.15 х 10−9 M compared to previous studies. In non-complementary DNA reactions, a minimal wavelength shift is detected, indicating the biosensor's high specificity, whereas studies using genomic DNAs from Leptospira and Clostridium difficile (as a control for genomic DNA) revealed similar observation. The integration of CQDs has achieved these remarkable biosensor traits, which hold great potential for developing a reliable leptospirosis diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Leptospira DNA
Leptospirosis
Carbon quantum dots
Tapered optical fiber
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber
Optical characterization of annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber
Detection of hybridized Leptospira DNA using annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of tapered optical fiber section.
Experimental setup of tapered optical fiber biosensor system.
The surface morphologies of (a) annealed CQDs, (b) thickness of CQDs, and (c) magnified annealed CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber.
EDS spectrum of (a) standard tapered optical fiber and (b) annealed CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber.
Raman shift of annealed CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber.
Schematic diagram of light propagation in annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber.
Transmission spectra before and after CQDs deposition (coating and annealing).
(a) FESEM, (b) EDS, and (c) AFM characterization of the hybridized cDNA on the annealed CQDs coated functionalized optical fiber.
The Raman spectrum of cDNA hybridization on the annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber.
Relationship between cDNA concentrations and wavelength shift using annealed CQDs.
Langmuir isotherm adsorption equation fitting of wavelength shift for annealed CQDs.
Dynamic range of hybridized cDNA corresponds to its wavelength shifts.
Specificity of the developed tapered optical fiber biosensor.
Specificity of the biosensor towards genomic DNA.
List of probe DNA and target DNA of rrs gene.

head.ce:title: Enhanced detection sensitivity of Leptospira DNA using a post-deposition annealed carbon quantum dots integrated tapered optical fiber biosensor

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Computer and Communication System

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Leptospirosis is an illness caused by a Leptospira bacterial infection, and it can be fatal if not correctly diagnosed and treated. This work explores the development of an optical biosensor that utilizes tapered optical fiber integrated with post-deposition annealed carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to improve Leptospira deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) detection. A standard optical single-mode fiber was tapered before being deposited with CQDs. The surface properties of CQDs on the tapered optical fiber were then tuned using a simple, one-step annealing process. Leptospira probe DNA was covalently bonded to the annealed CQDs surface using several commercially available linkers. The detection was achieved through probe DNA fragments that were hybridized into different concentrations of complementary target DNA. The biosensor limit of detection remained at 1.0 fM, but a better sensitivity of 2.321 ± 0.048 nm/nM was obtained. The affinity towards Leptospira complementary DNA improved as the equilibrium dissociation constant value was lowered to 0.15 х 10−9 M compared to previous studies. In non-complementary DNA reactions, a minimal wavelength shift is detected, indicating the biosensor's high specificity, whereas studies using genomic DNAs from Leptospira and Clostridium difficile (as a control for genomic DNA) revealed similar observation. The integration of CQDs has achieved these remarkable biosensor traits, which hold great potential for developing a reliable leptospirosis diagnosis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Leptospira is a pathogenic bacterium that causes the disease leptospirosis. It is an emerging infectious disease in humans with a worldwide distribution but is most common in countries with humid subtropical and tropical climates. Globally, leptospirosis causes an estimated 1.03 million human infections and 58,900 deaths annually [1]. Meanwhile, local data revealed that the incidence of leptospirosis increased from 1.03 cases in the year 2004 to 30.2 cases per 100,000 population in the year 2015 [2]. In accordance with the data, leptospirosis is on the rise and has become endemic in Malaysia. The Ministry of Health Malaysia also gazetted leptospirosis as a notifiable disease in 2010 under the Prevention and Control of Infectious Diseases Act 1988 [3].

With non-specific features of the symptom's presentation, clinical diagnosis to directly identify leptospirosis is challenging. This is why leptospirosis is known as “the great mimicker” due to overlapping manifestations with dengue, malaria, typhoid, and others [4]. Findings from the study suggest that leptospirosis is prevalent among dengue-negative febrile patients in Malaysia [5]. This scenario is concerning because the accurate diagnosis at the onset of symptoms shortly after infection is critical to initiate immediate specific antibiotic therapy for leptospirosis. Any missed or delayed diagnosis may progress from a mild illness to a severe one, eventually leading to death. Therefore, leptospirosis must be diagnosed promptly and accurately.

As leptospirosis is difficult to diagnose based solely on clinical features, specific laboratory tests must be performed. There are tests such as the microscopic agglutination test, which is known as the gold standard, and immunoglobin M enzyme-linked-immunosorbent assay, which is a genus-specific serological test that is commonly used for diagnosis of leptospirosis. Polymerase chain reaction, on the other hand, is a highly sensitive enzymatic amplification of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) that is efficient and use for early diagnosis. Though many diagnostic tests are available, their sensitivity and specificity vary, making accurate results untenable to acquire. The tests are also prone to producing false positives due to cross-reactivity and prior infections [6]. Given that some of these tests are costly and not widely available in every laboratory and hospital, they cannot be used as routine screening methods for leptospirosis. Hence, rapid diagnostic tests that detect infection in its early stages and aid in early treatment are required.

In response to the difficulties mentioned above, the development of biosensors has given rise to a plethora of alternative methods, such as optical, electrochemical, thermometric, piezoelectric, and magnetic biosensors. Among all these sensing methods, optical fiber biosensors have piqued the interest of researchers due to their anti-electromagnetic interference, small size, simple preparation, fast responses, and ease of integration. Optical fiber sensors have been widely used for health monitoring applications. For example, germanium-doped photosensitive optical fiber has been employed to detect ascorbic acid [7]. This study was critical for controlling the ascorbic acid level in human body. The optical fiber-based Mach-Zehnder interferometer (MZI) structure has also been used to sense collagen-IV [8]. Three layers of highly reflecting nanoparticles was coated on the sensing region, including polyvinyl-stabilised silver particles (PVA-AgNPs), gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and zinc-oxide nanoparticles (ZnO-NPs). Among the sensing layers, the best sensing performance was obtained on probes with PVA-AgNPs and ZnO-NPs. Surface plasmon resonance (SPR)-based optical fiber biosensors are also promising for detecting biomolecules for health monitoring. A hetero-core optical fiber sensor structure with AuNPs and PVA-AgNPs layers was used to induce the localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) phenomenon for the detection of l-Cysteine [9]. For cancer detection, B. Kaur et al. studied five types of cancerous cell lines, including Jurkat, HeLa, PC12, MDA-MB-231, MCF7, and normal cell lines, using a 2D materials-based fiber optic SPR biosensor [10]. Another way of inducing SPR involves using a tapered-in-tapered fibre (TiTF) probe with AuNPs and copper oxide nanoflowers (CuO-NFs). This developed sensor was evaluated through the investigation of its response to a number of p-cresol solution concentrations. Given these tried-and-true uses, optical fiber looks to be a good fit for the detection of other biomolecules like DNA and may have a big future in Leptospira DNA detection. In a standard optical fiber, light is confined in the core, whereas optical fiber sensing intends to have light interact with the surrounding medium. Thus, optical fiber needs to be physically modified for this type of application. By tapering the optical fiber, the diameter gradually decreases to a micron size. Subsequently, it enhances the formation of evanescence waves on the fiber's surface, which can be utilized for sensing.

The high sensitivity of optical fiber biosensors for Leptospira DNA detection is crucial for accurate disease diagnosis and reliable health monitoring. Thus, by improving the detection sensitivity of Leptospira DNA, accurate testing can be performed at low concentration. This kind of diagnostic tool is critically needed for mass screening of individuals during leptospirosis outbreaks to ensure fewer cases of unidentified infections. These days, high-sensitivity biomolecular detection is achieved by using light to interact with matter at the nanoscale. With an average diameter of under 10 nm, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a class of carbon nanomaterials, make an excellent mediator for nanophotonic sensing. CQDs have a high surface-to-volume ratio due to their small size, which can create many binding sites and enable more effective loading of biomolecules [11]. CQDs as a sensing layer also have better hydrophilicity, which then facilitates more hydroxyl groups to attach to DNA adsorption [12]. In general, CQDs serve as a driving signal-amplification technique in biosensing. By integrating CQDs on the optical fiber, more active sites are available for probe DNA binding. Subsequently, hybridization of more Leptospira DNA will lead to maximum optical response.

In addition, the most straightforward and efficient way to boost sensor sensitivity is post-deposition annealing. Through annealing, a high-density crystalline carbon structure with strong mechanical strength can be produced [13]. CQDs could also securely adhere to the optical fibre surface, and a more uniform coating can be formed. Moreover, annealing decreases the size of CQDs [14], resulting in a rougher sensing surface and a higher surface-to-volume ratio. This is an essential factor in sensor development as larger interaction area and more active sites will lead to higher measurable response, thus boosting sensitivity and LOD of a sensor.

As such, when this approach is integrated with the features of CQDs, great potential is foreseen to develop a biosensor that is much more sensitive, reliable, and stable for alternative leptospirosis diagnosis. The proposed biosensor is capable of rapid and accurate detection of Leptospira DNA, which is highly preferable compared to relying on observations of clinical symptoms or secondary markers such as platelet. Quantitative detection provided by this sensor also allows infection monitoring and can be applied for research purposes. Contaminated water is usually a major source of Leptospira infection. Therefore, by detecting traces of Leptospira in contaminated water using the proposed biosensor, the authorities can better delineate and monitor high-risk regions while alerting the public.

A standard single-mode optical fiber was initially modified by tapering its geometrical structure. The tapered section was formed by pulling the two ends of the optical fiber while a filament was heating it. Dimensions of the tapered section include a symmetric up-and-down taper transition with a length of 5 mm, a waist length of 15 mm, and a waist diameter of 12 mm (as shown in Fig. 1 ). The preceding dimension was chosen to ensure non-adiabatic light propagation to achieve the interferometric effect [15] and were also previously optimized by our earlier works in biomolecules detection [16]. That dimension has been successful in detecting proteins with avidin biorecognition molecules, antibodies with dengue E protein, and probe DNA with Leptospira target DNA [17–19].

Following fabrication, a glass slide was used to fix the tapered region of the optical fiber. The tapered fiber was then spliced into a single-mode pigtail on both ends. A broadband light source was connected to one end of the pigtail, and an optical spectrum analyzer (OSA) was connected to the other end for spectral observation, as shown in Fig. 2 .

Next, the fabricated tapered optical fiber was deposited with CQDs (provided by Qarbotech Sdn Bhd). The 10 μL of 1000 ppm of CQDs/distilled water solution was drop-casted on the tapered region. The solution was air-dried for about 5 min without turning off the light source at the launched power of 25 mW. The light source and OSA were disassembled once the CQDs deposited on the tapered region had thoroughly dried. The tapered optical fiber was then annealed in the oven at 70 °C temperature for 10 min. These annealing settings were used based on several prior successful studies on annealing the carbon nanomaterials on the optical fiber [20–22]. After the annealing procedure, the tapered optical fiber was fixed on a customized sample holder. The pigtail's two ends were reconnected to the light source and OSA to continue observing transmission spectra. The surface micro-characterization of annealed- CQDs tapered optical fiber was investigated using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The elemental composition of annealed-CQDs was determined using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) and their chemical structure was determined using Raman spectroscopy.

The annealed-CQDs surface will then need to be modified so that it is prepared to bond with the DNA probe. The modification of the surface was performed through the covalent bonding approach via hydroxylation using sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution, bio-modification using solution of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), and glutaraldehyde linking before binding with the probe DNA. The modification procedure was followed as in our previous work [18]. In brief, the tapered optical fiber was first hydroxylated for 45 min in 0.1 M NaOH to form a layer of an active hydroxyl group (-OH) on the annealed CQDs surface. After 45 min, the NaOH solution was drained out from the sample holder, and the tapered region was rinsed three times before the drying process. The hydroxylated CQDs-deposited tapered region surface was then silanized with a 2% (0.09 M) APTES for 45 min and subsequently rinsed and dried. The silanization yields an amine-terminated surface, which allows for conjugation with a linker before probe DNA adsorption. Next, the 2% glutaraldehyde solution (0.43 M), which acts as a linker, was used to activate the aminated surface for 60 min. The aldehyde-activated surface was rinsed and dried afterward. Then, immobilization of 1 μM probe DNA was performed for 60 min and followed by rinsing and drying. Table 1 displays the probe DNA of the rrs gene sequence that was designed according to the previous study [23], as well as their respective complementary DNA (cDNA) and non-complementary DNA (non-cDNA) targets.

At this point, the detection of Leptospira DNA was performed by immersing the target DNA for 60 min before being rinsed and dried. The DNA sample used for the targets was cDNA with varying concentrations of 0.1 fm to 1 μM and non-cDNA at 1 nM concentration for specificity testing. The sensor's specificity was then tested using genomic Leptospira at a concentration of 6.5 pg/ul as the target DNA. The pathogenic Leptospira interrogans serovars; Copenhageni and Canicola were studied for genomic DNA. Genomic DNA of Clostridium difficile, a bacterium that infects the human intestine, was used as negative control samples. The genomic DNA was denatured by thermally soaking it in 95 °C water for 2 min and rapidly cooling it in ice water before being introduced to probe DNA.

Each concentration of the experiment was conducted in triplicates set to ensure sensor reproducibility, and each test was performed with a freshly fabricated annealed-CQDs integrated sensor. It should be noted that deionized water was used in all rinsing steps, and a digital thermometer monitored the drying process at room temperature of 24 °C. After each immobilization and hybridization process was completed, the transmission response was recorded, and the output spectrum was evaluated by quantifying the wavelength shift. Before optical detection, FESEM and AFM were used to characterize the surface of Leptospira DNA on the annealed-CQDs tapered optical fiber, the elemental composition was investigated using EDS, and Raman spectroscopy was used to identify their chemical structure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 displays the FESEM images of annealed CQDs coated on the tapered optical fiber. On the surface of the optical fiber, CQDs were distributed uniformly, as shown in Fig. 3(a). Fig. 3(b) shows the average thickness layer of the CQDs deposited on the optical surface after annealing, which is 43.7 nm. The quasi-spherical grain structures with sizes ranging from 7.6 to 8.6 nm in the cross-section image of Fig. 3(c) represent the CQDs that were deposited and annealed onto the fiber's surface. In this work, the post-deposition annealing process resulted in smaller CQDs grain sizes than Zainuddin et al. (2023)., which utilized CQDs deposited on the fiber surface in their as-prepared state [24]. This phenomenon is consistent with the findings by Kulkarni et al. (2011)., who found that the carbon dots would become smaller as the annealing temperature was raised from 30 °C to 250–300 °C [25]. The overlapping assembly created by these nanostructures' deposition led to a larger surface area, which could facilitate the adsorption of DNA onto the sensing region. Therefore, more mode interference in light propagation and changes in the effective refractive index are anticipated, which would increase sensing sensitivity. These findings demonstrate the importance of post-deposition annealing in depositing CQDs to accentuate the distinct quality of the identical nanomaterial.

The topographies of annealed CQDs were also investigated using AFM spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4(a) showed annealed CQDs with a quasi-spherical shape and a dispersed distribution. From the AFM measurement, the root mean square (RMS) roughness values of the 2D and 3D topographies obtained are 9.714 nm and 41.288 nm, respectively. As mentioned in our previous study, the as-prepared CQDs coated on the tapered optical fiber have RMS roughness values of 7.195 nm and 39.538 nm with respect to the 2D and 3D topographies [8]. These values confirmed that the CQDs-coated surface got rougher following post-deposition annealing and was consistent with the FESEM morphologies. According to Rechendorf (2006), rough surfaces do promote the protein adsorption [26], which could then lead to enhanced sensing sensitivity. Thus, a rough surface with a larger surface area had a significant effect on the sensor response.

The EDS results of the standard and annealed CQDs coated on tapered optical fiber are shown in Fig. 5 . The changes in the percentages of the elements C, O, and Si were compared. The CQDs' prominent C peak of 45.41% suggested a high C content level. This confirmed that the optical fiber's thin film coating mainly consisted of CQDs. However, despite a decrease in weight percentage, the O element still displays a notable weight percentage. The O molecules on the CQDs play a crucial role in facilitating the functionalization process, affecting DNA adsorption.

The Raman spectrum of the annealed CQDs coated on the tapered optical fiber is displayed in Fig. 6 . Peaks at 1357 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 indicated the D and G bands, respectively. In general, the D band is associated with disordered C structure, whereas the G band is correlated to the in-plane stretching vibration mode of crystalline graphite carbon [27]. It was explicitly postulated that the majority of CQDs produced in this manner were mostly graphitic in nature due to the prominent peak of the G band in the Raman spectra [28]. Additionally, stronger G band intensity is also correlated with higher levels of graphitization [29]. High graphitization implies that the annealed CQDs are in a high crystallinity state, which results in high mechanical strength [13]. Consequently, annealed CQDs can be firmly coated over the tapered optical fiber region.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber
ce:section.ce:para:
The schematic diagram in Fig. 7 illustrates how light travels through annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber. The operation of non-adiabatic single-mode tapered fiber light propagation follows the same rules as a Mach-Zehnder interferometer. As transmitted light enters the down-taper region of the tapered fiber, due to the core size and V-number reduction, high-order modes are excited at the tapered region. Combining modes at the tapering region results in an increase in the portion of the evanescent waves. The interaction between the tapered optical fiber's surrounding environment and evanescent waves on its surface underlies the sensor's operating principle. Upon reaching the up-taper region, output light intensity, I, is governed by the following equation [15]:
(1)
I =
I 1
+
I 2
+ 2
I 1
I 2
cos ⁡
(
Δ Φ
)
where I 1 and I 2 are the intensity of coupling modes (fundamental mode and high-order mode) with a phase shift between the two modes, ΔΦ which can be expressed as:
(2)
Δ Φ =
[
2 π
(
Δ
n
e f f
)
L w
λ
]
where Δn eff is the difference between the effective refractive index of the core and the external surrounding (
n c
e f f
−
n
e x t
e f f
) along the waist length of the tapered region, L w. λ is the input wavelength of the light.

According to Equation (2), any changes in the cladding refractive index of the tapered region will influence Δneff and, subsequently, ΔΦ, which then ultimately cause a quantifiable wavelength shift in the output spectrum. This was observed in Fig. 8 as a redshift phenomenon following post-deposition annealed CQDs onto the tapered region. The transmission spectrum that was observed induced a wavelength shift of 0.910 nm. The annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber has a longer redshift than standard tapered optical fiber, suggesting that light propagation occurs at a lower photon energy due to their rougher interface [30]. Therefore, the findings showed an optical interference consequence at the tapered region, proving that the CQDs were successfully coated and annealed onto the tapered optical fiber.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of hybridized Leptospira DNA using annealed CQDs integrated tapered optical fiber

ce:section.ce:para: The surface of the annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber after hybridization of cDNA appeared to have a scattered strand structure, as shown by FESEM analysis in Fig. 9 (a). This validated cDNA hybridization reaction was present on the sensor surface. According to P. Vengadesh et al. (2015)., their work also suggested a similar structure that demonstrated DNA adsorption along the cut of conductive wires [31]. The elemental composition of hybridized cDNA obtained from EDS is shown in Fig. 9(b). As depicted in the figure, after the cDNA hybridization, higher weight percentages of C (63.39%), lower weight percentages of O (25.38%), and Si (11.23%) were revealed. These results are consistent with Mustapha Kamil et al. (2018)., who also reported that when the dengue virus E-protein was attached to tapered fiber, the C weight percentage increased, and O and Si weight percentages decreased [17]. In conclusion, the increasing C and decreasing O imply that cDNA hybridizations have successfully generated a layer of hybridized DNA on the annealed CQDs surface. In contrast, the additional layer made it harder to detect the Si element from the optical fiber substrate. Next, Fig. 9(c) shows the AFM images of hybridized DNA on the annealed CQDs surface. The CQDs' spherical shapes indicated some degeneration following DNA hybridization. This occurrence suggested that there may have been interfaces between DNA attachments and the surface of annealed CQDs. AFM measurement was used to calculate the image's degree of roughness. The measured RMS values for the 2D and 3D roughness are 21.839 nm and 111.880 nm, respectively. It is evident that DNA adsorption upon hybridization results in a higher roughness value. As a result of DNA adsorption, the effective refractive index was increased that affects the light propagation property and its wavelength shift.

Fig. 10 displays the Raman shift of hybridized cDNA on the annealed CQDs surface. Based on the appearance of the Raman bands, the D and G bands were observed to remain. However, the D band intensity was higher than the G band. The integrated intensity ratio ID/IG for the D band and G band is widely used for describing the defect amount in the graphitic materials [32]. Raman bands at 618 cm⁻1 and 931 cm⁻1 peaks were ascribed to the DNA bases, which were cytosine and guanine, respectively [33]. 804 cm⁻1 was attributed to the wag of C–H on CC bonds on the thymine DNA bases [34]. This strong band was likely to appear due to the abundance of DNA bases on the sensing surface. At 1066 cm⁻1, the phosphate backbone (PO4⁻) band was foreseen to appear [35]. A band at 1203 cm⁻1 was assigned to vibrations involving in-plane ring vibrations of the cytosine [35]. Small peaks appeared at 1374 cm⁻1 and 1376 cm⁻1 bands, which indicate the presence of the DNA bases of thymine and adenine, respectively [36]. The band at 1465 cm⁻1 was attributed to the guanine DNA base [37]. The band at 1616 cm⁻1 was assigned to CO stretching of the guanine [38]. All these bands confirmed that the cDNA is successfully hybridized with probe DNA on the annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber.

Fig. 11 depicts the spectra comparison of optical response following chemical modification of annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber, probe DNA immobilization, and cDNA hybridization. The redshift observed in the transmission spectra established the occurrence of successful cDNA hybridization. The peak spectrum of immobilized probe DNA shifted to the longer wavelength by about 2.075 nm compared to the peak spectrum of annealed CQDs/surface modification. After the peak spectrum of the probe DNA, the peak spectrum of the hybridized cDNA is further shifted to the longer wavelength by 3.545 nm. The redshift of the peak spectrum proves that light propagation is changed. It is owing to a change in the effective RI of the tapered optical fiber caused by the successful binding of targeted cDNA. The adsorption of hybridized cDNA was also related to the localized photon energy bound. Following hybridization, energy absorption increases, but photon energy decreases. Consequently, it causes the frequency of light to reduce, and the transmission spectrum shifts to longer wavelengths [30].

Sensitivity is a crucial metric for evaluating the performance of the biosensor. The sensitivity of this biosensor was assessed by its response to various concentrations of cDNA (0.1 nM–1.0 nM). This concentration range was chosen because, as reported in our earlier study [18], it shows the effective sensing region by a higher magnitude of wavelength shift when a wide dynamic range of concentration (0.1 fM to 1 M) was used. Thus, a similar range was chosen for sensitivity comparison. Fig. 12 shows the linear relationship between the concentration of cDNA and its corresponding wavelength shifts. The sensitivity of the annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber biosensor was 2.321 ± 0.048 nm/nM with excellent linearity of more than 99%. The sensitivity value was increased by approximately 21% compared to the as-prepared CQDs tapered optical fiber biosensor (1.8295 ± 0.6 nm/nM) [24].

Furthermore, affinity is a crucial factor in influencing the efficiency of the biosensor. It typically can be determined by measuring the equilibrium dissociation constant (K d) of the biosensor. The K d value is then used to quantify and rank order the strength of bio-molecular interactions. The lower the K d value, the more strongly the ligand binds to the receptor. Thus, to assess the biosensor's affinity, a set of wavelength shifts towards Leptospira cDNA data was fitted to the Langmuir adsorption isotherm equation. Fig. 13 shows the achieved K d of the biosensor, which is 0.15 × 10−9 with R2 = 0.987 and an average standard deviation of ±0.04. With this value, the K d, using annealed CQDs as the sensing layer, was significantly lower than several other DNA biosensors. For example, K d for Leptospira DNA detection using the as-prepared CQDs tapered optical fiber sensor was 0.552 х 10−9 M [24]. Within the comparable length of DNA bases, the SPR-based biosensor by F. Kröner et al. (2016). reported that their K d value was 0.73 × 10−09 ± 0.12 M [39]. Also, for electrochemical biosensors; L. Roon and A. J. Bonham (2018) reported their K d was 0.9 × 10−9 ± 0.2 M [40] and L. Huang et al. (2013). reported that their K d was 0.91 × 10−06 M [41]. These findings imply that annealed CQDs tapered optical fiber efficiently enhanced the affinity for Leptospira DNA detection.

Next, the limit of detection (LOD) was determined by comparing the wavelength shift of cDNA over a range of Leptospira DNA concentrations (0 M–1 μM), as shown in Fig. 14 . According to Fig. 14, there was minimal difference in wavelength shifts at lower concentrations (0 M− 0.1 nM) compared to higher concentrations (0.1 nM - 1.0 M). This was anticipated because there were more cDNA strands present to construct the duplex DNA formation, and increasing the concentration of cDNA normally increases DNA hybridization. This situation allows for adequate detection and more obvious wavelength shift. Yet, there were fewer cDNA strands to hybridize with the probe DNA at lower cDNA concentrations. The reduced DNA hybridization has eventually resulted in an ambiguous wavelength shift [42]. As the 0.1 fM wavelength shift is indistinguishable from 0 M (control concentration), the LOD for this biosensor is established at 1.0 fM concentration. This work's LOD is superior to other reported DNA biosensors that use nanomaterial coating. For example, optical biosensor by L. Li et al. (2022), 1 pM [43] and L. Wang et al. (2023), 0.2 pM [44] and electrical-based sensor by M. Bahri et al. (2023), 57 fM [45] and K. Avelino et al. (2022), 1.34 fM [46].

In comparison to other transducers for Leptospira DNA detection, the measured fM-range LOD in this work is equivalent to the value reported in SPR evanescent wave sensor targeting Leptospira sec-Y gene [47]. The LOD of 1.0 fM concentration corresponds to 6.4 fg/mL, which is higher than the LOD of Leptospira DNA detection using the PCR protocol (23.1 pg/μL) [48].The LOD is also below the Leptospira DNA level found in human urine using the DNA extraction method (6.131 ng/μL) [49].

To sum up, the inclusion of CQDs with further post-deposition annealing on the sensor surface has significantly enhanced the detection of Leptospira DNA by achieving higher sensitivity, higher affinity, and low LOD. This is because, in addition to the high surface area and more binding active sites offered by CQDs, annealing the CQDs surface improves coating adherence to the optical fiber. Also, as discussed in Section 3.1, annealing reduces the size of the CQDs, making the sensing surface rougher. All these factors contribute to the increment of DNA adsorption on the sensor surface. As a result, the DNA adsorption rate increased and boosted DNA hybridization. As a result, the DNA adsorption rate increases and boosts DNA hybridization. Consequentially, the wavelength shift is increased, and the multivalent approach of the binding affinity is strengthened [50].

The most essential characteristic of a biosensor is its specificity. It refers to a bioreceptor's ability to detect a specific target analyte molecule. Specific detection is acquired when the hydrogen bonds between the base pairs of the cDNA and the probe DNA are complementary and successfully form a duplex. For this investigation, Leptospira cDNA and non-cDNA were utilized at the same concentration of 1.0 nM to achieve a valid comparison. Sterile ultrapure water, used as a DNA buffer, was set as a reference at a concentration of 0 M. The optical response for the biosensor's specificity is shown in Fig. 15 . From the histogram, only slight wavelength shifts were observed when the sensors were tested with non-cDNA and sterile ultrapure water. Contrarily, the wavelength shift with cDNA was significantly stronger than others. These findings indicate that the anticipated sensor has a high specificity towards the Leptospira DNA.

The specificity was also investigated by observing the wavelength shift towards extracted genomic DNA hybridized with Leptospira probe DNA. Along with extracted DNA samples of Leptospira interrogans serovar, Copenhageni, and Canicola, the extracted DNA of the Clostridium difficile bacteria, which infects the human intestine, was used as a non-target control. As in Fig. 16 , the graphs show that both Leptospira serovars induced a significant wavelength shift compared to the Clostridium difficile. At a concentration of 6.5 pg/μL, Canicola and Copenhageni achieved wavelength shifts of 1.897 nm and 1.958 nm, respectively, while Clostridium difficile achieved a shift of 0.153 nm. These results verified that this biosensor can detect extracted DNA of Leptospira at a lower concentration than the biosensor developed by E. Dester et al. (2022), which can only detect extracted DNA of E. coli O157 at the lowest concentration of 2.5 ng/μL [51].

However, the wavelength shifts of Leptospira-extracted DNA appear to be much smaller than that of synthetic cDNA, as 6.5 pg/μL is equivalent to 1 nM. It happened because the lengthy genome itself has a crowding effect on the sensing region, causing steric hindrance and, as a result, relatively low binding effectiveness. Nevertheless, the post-deposition annealing of CQDs helps to improve the target detection efficiency of tapered optical fiber biosensors. In this work, the wavelength shift was successfully increased by roughly 45% relative to the tapered optical fiber with no nanomaterial coating [18] and by roughly 20% compared to the integration of tapered optical fiber with the as-prepared CQDs [24]. The data conclusively demonstrate an improvement in the sensor's ability to detect longer genomic DNA sequences in actual samples and differentiate Leptospira DNA from other bacterial species DNA.

In this work, the CQDs were reduced in size by post-deposition annealing, which led to an increment in surface roughness and the surface-to-volume ratio while also strengthening the adhesiveness to the surface of tapered optical fiber. With these superior features, the integration of tapered optical fiber with post-deposition annealing of CQDs managed to elevate Leptospira DNA adsorption, consequently enhancing optical sensing performance. Remarkably, the proposed biosensor exhibited a sensitivity of 2.321 ± 0.048 nm/nM with excellent linearity of more than 99%. The biosensor affinity towards Leptospira cDNA also improves as the K d value was lowered to 0.15 х 10−9 M compared to the as-prepared CQDs functionalized biosensor, despite the biosensor LOD remaining at 1.0 fM. When it comes to non-cDNA reactions, a minimal wavelength shift is detected, indicating the biosensor's high specificity. Meanwhile, the investigations using the two genomic DNAs of Leptospira and Clostridium difficile (as a control for genomic DNA) also revealed high specificity. The findings highlight the importance of optimizing the sensing layer through post-deposition annealing in the development of CQDs-integrated optical sensor. The elevated sensitivity reported in this work will be highly beneficial in future leptospirosis diagnoses and environmental detection. This study also exhibits the potential and feasibility of optical fiber as a highly sensitive, rapid, and compact biosensing transducer. The developed optical fiber biosensor is still limited to one biomarker pattern per test, and the design also necessitates the use of bulky equipment to conduct the detection. The forthcoming innovation in optical fiber biosensor technology will demand the design of sensing regions capable of multiple biomarker patterns analyses on the same transducer. Additionally, the innovation could engineer a compact and portable device with automation software and a microfluidic sample case. The realization of such device will result in a more practical biosensor with in-situ capability and simple operation for untrained users. With this development, it will be possible to achieve a wider distribution of the sensor, especially in rural corners of the world where the infection is more prevalent.

Nurul Hida Zainuddin: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Preparation. Hui Yee Chee: Resources, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Suraya Abdul Rashid: Resources. Muhammad Zamharir Ahmad: Resources. Zuraidah Zan: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Hafiz Abu Bakar: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Mohammed Thamer Alresheedi: Funding acquisition. Mohd Adzir Mahdi: Methodology, Supervision. Mohd Hanif Yaacob: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the Ministry of Higher Education Malaysia under Grant Number PRGS/2/2019/STG05/UPM/03/1 and the King Saud University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, under Researchers Supporting Project (RSP2023R336).

Oliveira, “Electrochemical DNA biosensor for chronic myelocytic leukemia based on hybrid nanostructure,” Bioelectrochemistry, vol. 147, no. February, 2022, doi: 10.1016/j.bioelechem.2022.108176.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fiber-based surface plasmon resonance biosensor for DNA detection
IEEE Sensor. J.

Rev. Soc. Bras. Med. Trop.

Thong, “A duplex endpoint PCR assay for rapid detection and differentiation of Leptospira strains,” Rev. Soc. Bras. Med. Trop., vol. 50, no. 2, pp. 239-242, 2017, doi: 10.1590/0037-8682-0364-2016.

Heal. Sci. J. Indones.

Indones., vol. 11, no. 2, pp. 77-84, 2020, doi: 10.22435/hsji.v11i2.3749.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of unamplified E. coli O157 DNA extracted from large food samples using a gold nanoparticle colorimetric biosensor
Biosensors
